meta.title: Future-Proof Engineer Roadmap
meta.tagline: From 'what even is a computer' to a paid AI engineer — a roadmap you actually finish.
meta.intended_audience: A-Levels student (16–18) preparing for university, planning a career in software/AI in an AI-saturated job market.
meta.total_estimated_months: 24–36 months at ~10–15 hrs/week, faster if full-time
philosophy: Build > Watch. Every phase ends with a thing you made.
Vocabulary first. You can't learn what you can't name.
Fundamentals are the moat. AI can write a React component. AI cannot replace someone who deeply understands systems, math, and product judgment.
Earn early. By month 12 you should be making money — freelance, internships, or selling small products.
Public learning. Push every project to GitHub. Write short posts. Recruiters Google you.

future_proof_domains.intro: AI will eat repetitive coding. It will not eat the engineer who designs the system, debugs the weird edge case, owns the data pipeline, secures the infra, or builds the product. Below are the IT roles most likely to thrive — not survive — in 2030 and beyond.
future_proof_domains.primary_recommendation: AI / ML Engineering (with deep CS fundamentals)
future_proof_domains.why_primary: AI itself is the single biggest industry being built right now. The people who build, train, deploy, secure, and integrate AI systems are the most leveraged engineers on Earth. Compensation is the highest in tech, demand is growing, and the field is too young to have hit any ceiling. Even when AI writes most code, someone has to design the AI that writes the code.

domains.name: AI / ML Engineering
domains.outlook: Highest demand role through 2030+. Salaries 1.5–3x typical software roles.
domains.what_you_do: Build, train, fine-tune, and deploy ML models — LLMs, recommendation systems, computer vision.

domains.name: ML Infrastructure / MLOps
domains.what_you_do: Build the systems that train, serve, and monitor ML models at scale.
domains.why_safe: AI cannot debug a Kubernetes cluster melting down at 3am.

domains.name: AI Safety, Alignment & Evaluation
domains.outlook: Newest hot field. Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind hiring aggressively.
domains.what_you_do: Test models, build guardrails, study how models fail, research interpretability.
domains.why_safe: As models get more powerful, the bottleneck is trust, not capability.

domains.name: Cybersecurity (AI-augmented)
domains.what_you_do: Defend systems, pen-test, build secure cloud architecture.
domains.why_safe: Adversarial — there is always a human attacker to outsmart.

domains.name: Robotics & Embedded AI
domains.outlook: Massive boom coming as humanoid robots leave research labs.
domains.what_you_do: Combine ML with the physical world — drones, robots, self-driving.
domains.why_safe: Physical world is messy and slow to automate.

domains.name: Data Engineering
domains.what_you_do: Build pipelines that move and transform massive amounts of data into ML-ready form.
domains.why_safe: Foundational, every AI company needs them.

domains.name: AI Product Management / Applied AI
domains.what_you_do: Decide what AI products to build, how, for whom.
domains.why_safe: Requires judgment, communication, taste — areas where AI is weakest.

phases.title: Phase 0 — What Even Is a Computer?
phases.objective: Build the vocabulary. Before code, before AI, before anything — understand what a computer is, what software is, what 'the cloud' actually means.
phases.outcome: You can explain to a non-technical friend what happens when they press a key, open a website, or close a laptop. You're no longer scared of the words.

lessons.title: What's inside a computer? CPU, RAM, storage, GPU
lessons.objective: Understand the 5 essential parts of every computer and what each does.

reading.name: Crash Course Computer Science Ep 1–3 (free YouTube)

lessons.project: Open pcpartpicker.com. Design a $700 PC. Write 1 sentence per part explaining what it does.

lessons.title: Bits, bytes, and how computers store everything
lessons.objective: Why binary (0s and 1s) underlies text, images, music, video — everything.

reading.name: Code by Charles Petzold (book — chapters 1–4 are mind-blowing)

lessons.project: Spell your first name in binary using ASCII (look up the table). Then in hex. No computer needed — just paper.

lessons.title: What's a phone, a laptop, a server? They're all computers
lessons.objective: Understand that the 'server' running Instagram is just a beefy computer in a warehouse.
lessons.project: Take 5 different things you use today (phone, ATM, microwave, car infotainment, smart TV). For each: what's the CPU doing right now?

modules.title: Software: programs that bring hardware alive

lessons.title: What is software? Operating systems, apps, websites
lessons.objective: Software = instructions. The OS manages everything else.

reading.name: Crash Course CS Ep 18 — Operating Systems

lessons.project: Make a list of all software running on your phone right now. Group them: OS-level vs apps you opened.

lessons.title: Files, folders, and how data is organized
lessons.objective: Everything you save lives in a file. Files live in folders. Get fluent with this.
lessons.project: Reorganize your Downloads folder into 4 subfolders: Documents, Images, Videos, Other. Move every file. Don't delete anything.

lessons.title: What is the internet? (it's not just WiFi)
lessons.objective: The internet is cables in the ocean. WiFi is the last 30 feet. Big difference.

reading.name: How the Internet Works in 5 minutes

lessons.project: Look up where the cable from your country to the rest of the world physically lands. Most countries: 1–3 cables. Surprising, right?

lessons.title: What happens when you type google.com?
lessons.objective: DNS, IP addresses, requests. The single most-asked question in tech interviews.
lessons.project: In your own words, write a 1-page explanation of what happens when you load youtube.com. No copy-paste.

lessons.title: What is 'the cloud'? It's just someone else's computer
lessons.objective: Cloud = renting computers from Amazon/Google/Microsoft instead of owning them. That's literally it.

reading.name: What is cloud computing? (AWS in plain English)

lessons.project: Pick 5 apps you use (Netflix, Instagram, Spotify…). Where does each store its data? Hint: AWS, GCP, or Azure.

lessons.title: App vs website vs SaaS vs platform — the vocabulary
lessons.objective: These words confuse beginners. After this lesson, they won't.
lessons.project: Make a 2-column table: 5 things you use that are 'just websites' vs 5 that are 'apps'. What's the actual difference?

phases.title: Phase 1 — Your First Lines of Code (Python)
phases.objective: Write your first program. Then your tenth. Python is the friendliest language and also the language of AI — perfect choice.
phases.outcome: You've written 5 small programs that actually do something. You're not scared of code anymore.

lessons.title: Get coding without installing anything: Replit
lessons.objective: Don't waste a week installing Python. Use Replit in your browser, code immediately.
lessons.project: Sign up for Replit. Create a Python project. Write a program that prints 'Hello, [your name]!' three times. That's it. Take a screenshot.

lessons.title: What is programming? Variables, types, print
lessons.objective: A variable is a labeled box. Types tell the computer what kind of thing is in the box.

courses.title: 100 Days of Code: The Complete Python Pro Bootcamp (Angela Yu)
Python for Everybody (University of Michigan)

lessons.project: Write a program that asks for your name and birth year, then prints 'Hi NAME, you are X years old.'

lessons.title: Math, comparisons, booleans
lessons.objective: Computers are calculators with feelings. Master arithmetic and true/false.
lessons.project: Build a simple tip calculator: ask for bill total and number of people, print each person's share including 15% tip.

lessons.title: If, else, elif — making decisions
lessons.objective: Programs make choices. This is how.
lessons.project: Build a 'is it a leap year?' checker. Ask for a year, print yes/no using the leap year rule.

lessons.title: While and for loops — doing things repeatedly
lessons.objective: Computers love repetition. You'll save thousands of hours mastering loops.
lessons.project: Build rock-paper-scissors vs the computer. Best of 5. Track score.

lessons.title: Functions — packaging logic for reuse
lessons.objective: Don't repeat yourself. Wrap repeated logic in a function.
lessons.project: Make a calculator with separate functions for add, subtract, multiply, divide. Menu in a loop.

phases.title: Phase 2 — Real Programming
phases.objective: Step up from toy programs to real ones. Lists, files, errors, classes. The skills you actually use every day.
phases.outcome: You've built 3 multi-file programs that read/write data and don't crash on bad input.

lessons.title: Lists — ordered collections
lessons.objective: Most data comes in lists. Master them.
lessons.project: Build a to-do list program. Add, remove, mark done, show all. In-memory is fine.

lessons.title: Dictionaries — key-value pairs
lessons.objective: When you need to look things up by name, use a dict. Most-used data structure in Python.
lessons.project: Build a contact book using a dict. Add, search by name, update, delete.

lessons.title: Sets and tuples (briefly)
lessons.objective: When uniqueness matters or order shouldn't change.
lessons.project: Given a paragraph of text, find every unique word and how many times each appears.

lessons.title: Reading and writing files
lessons.objective: Programs that don't lose your data when they close. Major upgrade.

courses.title: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python (Al Sweigart)

lessons.project: Upgrade your to-do app: now it saves to and loads from a tasks.txt file.

lessons.title: Error handling — try/except
lessons.objective: Real programs handle bad input gracefully instead of crashing.
lessons.project: Take your tip calculator. Make it never crash, no matter what the user types.

lessons.title: JSON — the language of data on the web
lessons.objective: Every API speaks JSON. So do you, now.
lessons.project: Save your contact book as a contacts.json file instead of a plain text file.

lessons.title: Classes and objects — modeling things in code
lessons.objective: Most professional code is organized in classes. This is the dominant paradigm.
lessons.project: Build a tiny text-RPG: Player and Enemy classes. Combat system. 5+ enemies, 3 weapons.

phases.title: Phase 3 — The Engineer's Toolkit
phases.objective: Every professional engineer uses these tools daily: terminal, Git, GitHub, a real IDE, and AI helpers. None of them are optional.
phases.outcome: You can navigate a computer via the keyboard alone, save your code with Git, share it on GitHub, debug like a pro, and use AI as a coding partner without it doing the thinking for you.

modules.title: The terminal — talking to your computer directly

lessons.title: Why engineers use the terminal
lessons.objective: It's faster, scriptable, and how every server talks. Click less, type more.
lessons.project: Open a terminal (Mac/Linux) or PowerShell (Windows). Make a folder called 'practice', go into it, create 3 files, list them. Don't touch the mouse.

lessons.title: Essential commands: cd, ls, mkdir, mv, cp, rm, cat, grep
lessons.objective: The 10 commands you'll type a million times in your career.

courses.title: Linux Mastery: The Linux Command Line in 11.5 Hours (Ziyad Yehia)

lessons.project: Without using your mouse, organize a folder of 50 mixed files into Images/, Docs/, and Other/ subfolders. Document each command you used.

modules.title: Git & GitHub — saving and sharing your code

lessons.title: What is Git? Why every coder uses it
lessons.objective: Git is time travel for your code. Made a mistake? Roll back. Working with others? No more emailing zip files.
lessons.project: Install Git. Create a folder. Run `git init`. Add a file. Commit it. Change it. Commit again. Run `git log`. Marvel.

lessons.title: Commits, branches, merges
lessons.objective: Branches let you experiment without breaking the main code. Merges combine work.

reading.name: Learn Git Branching (interactive game — best Git tutorial ever)

courses.title: The Git & GitHub Bootcamp (Colt Steele)

lessons.project: Complete the Learn Git Branching interactive tutorial all the way through.

lessons.title: GitHub — your code on the internet
lessons.objective: GitHub is where the world's code lives. Your portfolio, every job application, every open source project.

reading.name: How to write a great README

lessons.project: Sign up on GitHub. Push your contact book and to-do list projects to public repos. Write a real README for each.

lessons.title: Pull requests & contributing to others' code
lessons.objective: How real teams ship software together. Also how you contribute to open source.
lessons.project: Find any beginner-friendly open source repo (good-first-issue tag). Open a PR — even a typo fix in the README counts. Welcome to open source.

lessons.title: VS Code — the editor everyone uses
lessons.objective: Free, fast, extensible. Most-used code editor on Earth.
lessons.project: Install VS Code. Install the Python and GitLens extensions. Open one of your earlier projects and run it from inside VS Code.

lessons.title: Debugging like a pro
lessons.objective: When code doesn't work, you don't guess. You debug. Breakpoints, print debugging, reading error messages carefully.

reading.name: How to debug in VS Code

lessons.project: Take a project you've built. Intentionally break it 3 times in 3 different ways. Use the VS Code debugger to find each bug. Document your process.

lessons.title: Reading documentation (a superpower)
lessons.objective: Docs feel intimidating. Once you can read them, you can learn anything.
lessons.project: Pick a Python library you've never used (e.g., `requests`). Using ONLY its docs, write a program that fetches a webpage and prints the title.

lessons.title: Asking for help: Stack Overflow + Discord + AI
lessons.objective: Engineers don't memorize. They look up. Mastering this is half the job.

reading.name: How to ask a good question (Stack Overflow)

lessons.project: Join 2 Discord communities for programming beginners. Ask one real question (after trying for 10 min yourself). Help answer one beginner question.

lessons.title: Using Claude / ChatGPT / Copilot to learn faster
lessons.objective: AI is the best tutor in the world if you use it right. The wrong way: copy-paste solutions. The right way: ask it to explain.

reading.name: How to use AI for learning code, not avoiding it

courses.title: ChatGPT Prompt Engineering for Developers

lessons.project: Re-do one of your earlier projects WITH AI as a tutor. Rule: never copy code blindly. Ask it to explain every line you don't understand. Compare with how long the project took the first time.

lessons.title: When AI is wrong (and how to spot it)
lessons.objective: AI confidently lies. If you can't tell when, you'll ship broken code.
lessons.project: Ask Claude/ChatGPT 5 questions about Python where you already know the answer. Try to catch it being subtly wrong. Document any errors.

phases.earning_milestone: MILESTONE: After this phase you can credibly call yourself a junior dev. With Python + Git + GitHub + a portfolio of 4–5 small repos, you can already start getting tutoring gigs (helping younger students learn Python) for ₹500–2000/hr in your local area, or on platforms like Preply.

phases.title: Phase 4 — Databases: Where Data Lives
phases.objective: Almost every app has a database. Understand SQL, schemas, and how to make data persist beyond your program.
phases.outcome: You can design a database schema, write SQL queries, and build a Python app backed by a real database.

lessons.title: What is a database? Why not just use files?
lessons.objective: Databases handle: many users at once, fast lookup, complex relationships, durability. Files don't.

reading.name: What is a database? (Oracle plain English)

lessons.project: Pick 5 apps you use. For each, list 3 things they store in a database. (Instagram: posts, users, likes…)

lessons.title: Relational vs NoSQL — the two big families
lessons.objective: Relational (SQL): structured tables. NoSQL: flexible documents. Each has its uses.
lessons.project: For 5 different scenarios (banking, Twitter, Spotify playlists, file storage, real-time gaming), decide: SQL or NoSQL? Defend each pick.

lessons.title: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — the four verbs
lessons.objective: These four operations cover 95% of database work. Get fluent.

courses.title: The Ultimate MySQL Bootcamp (Colt Steele)

lessons.project: Complete every exercise on SQLBolt.

lessons.title: JOINs — connecting tables
lessons.objective: The hardest, most-asked SQL concept. Once you get it, you can answer any data question.

reading.name: A visual explanation of SQL JOINs

lessons.project: Solve all the JOIN exercises on SQLZoo or LeetCode SQL easy section (~10 problems).

lessons.title: Designing a schema — the art of database modeling
lessons.objective: Bad schema = forever pain. Learn to design before you code.
lessons.project: Design (on paper) a schema for a small library: books, members, loans, fines. List every table, column, type, and relationship.

lessons.title: SQLite — the database in a single file
lessons.objective: Perfect for small apps and learning. Ships with Python.
lessons.project: Rewrite your contact book as a Python program backed by SQLite. Persists between runs.

lessons.title: PostgreSQL — the production-grade SQL database
lessons.objective: What real companies use. Get familiar early.
lessons.project: Install Postgres locally OR use a free Supabase account. Migrate your contact-book schema. Run 5 useful queries.

lessons.title: MongoDB — your first NoSQL database
lessons.objective: Documents instead of tables. Useful for flexible / nested data.
lessons.project: Free MongoDB Atlas account. Insert 100 fake user documents. Write 5 queries (find, filter, update, sort, aggregate).

phases.title: Phase 5 — Building Things People See (Frontend)
phases.objective: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — what every webpage on Earth is built from. By the end, you can build websites that look great.
phases.outcome: Two deployed websites: a personal portfolio and a working interactive app.

lessons.title: HTML basics — the skeleton of every webpage
lessons.objective: Tags, elements, attributes. The 'nouns' of the web.

courses.title: The Web Developer Bootcamp 2024 (Colt Steele)
The Odin Project — full free fullstack curriculum

lessons.project: Build a static personal page about yourself. 1 page, no styling yet.

lessons.title: CSS — making things beautiful
lessons.objective: Colors, fonts, spacing, layout. The 'art' of web dev.

reading.name: CSS Tricks — A complete guide to flexbox

lessons.project: Style your personal page. Make it actually nice. Mobile responsive.

lessons.title: Responsive design — looks good on phones too
lessons.objective: Most users are on mobile. Don't make them pinch-zoom.
lessons.project: Open your personal page on your phone. Fix everything broken. Use Chrome devtools mobile mode.

lessons.title: Cloning a real site to lock in skills
lessons.objective: Pick a brand. Rebuild their homepage. The fastest way to level up.
lessons.project: Clone the homepage of Stripe, Linear, or Apple. Mobile-responsive. No copy-paste of their code — recreate from scratch.

lessons.title: JavaScript fundamentals
lessons.objective: Variables, functions, arrays — basically Python with weird syntax. The most-used language on Earth.

reading.name: javascript.info (free book — best on the internet)

courses.title: The Complete JavaScript Course (Jonas Schmedtmann)

lessons.project: Build a tip calculator as a webpage with HTML + JS. Update result as user types.

lessons.title: DOM manipulation — code that changes the page
lessons.objective: How JavaScript talks to HTML. The core of interactivity.
lessons.project: Build a to-do list webpage. Add items, mark done, delete. All interactive.

lessons.title: Fetch and APIs — pulling data from the internet
lessons.objective: Most modern apps fetch data from servers. Time to do that yourself.
lessons.project: Build a weather app. User types a city, page shows the weather. Use OpenWeatherMap free API.

phases.title: Phase 6 — Modern Frontend with React
phases.objective: React is the dominant UI framework. The most-asked-for skill on every frontend job listing.
phases.outcome: Two React apps deployed to a public URL.

lessons.title: Why React? Components and the virtual DOM
lessons.objective: Build UIs as reusable Lego blocks. Once you click, you'll never go back to vanilla JS.

reading.name: React official tutorial — Tic Tac Toe (best React intro)

courses.title: React - The Complete Guide (Maximilian Schwarzmüller)

lessons.project: Complete the official React Tic Tac Toe tutorial.

lessons.title: Components, props, state
lessons.objective: The three concepts you'll use 1000 times a day in React.
lessons.project: Build a movie tracker: search via TMDB free API, mark watched, save favorites.

lessons.title: Hooks: useState, useEffect
lessons.objective: How modern React handles state and side effects.
lessons.project: Build a Pomodoro timer. Customizable durations. Saves history of completed sessions.

lessons.title: Styling React: Tailwind CSS
lessons.objective: Tailwind is the dominant styling approach in 2026. Fast, consistent, awesome.
lessons.project: Restyle your movie tracker with Tailwind. Make it look like a real product.

phases.earning_milestone: MILESTONE: At ~12–14 months in, you can take small frontend freelance gigs. Going rates: $15–40/hr to start, $50–80/hr after a few projects under your belt. Open profiles on Upwork, Fiverr, Toptal (later), and your local equivalent.

phases.title: Phase 7 — Backend & Full-Stack Apps
phases.objective: Build the server side. APIs, authentication, full-stack apps where everything you've learned comes together.
phases.outcome: One full-stack app you'd be proud to demo. Deployed. Real users could sign up.

lessons.title: What is a backend? What's a server?
lessons.objective: The part of an app you can't see — but everything depends on.
lessons.project: Draw on paper how Instagram works end-to-end: phone app → server → database → back to phone.

lessons.title: HTTP, REST, and APIs
lessons.objective: The conversation protocol between any two computers on the web.
lessons.project: Use Postman or curl to call 3 different public APIs (GitHub, OpenWeather, Pokémon). Make a table of each: endpoint, method, what you got back.

lessons.title: FastAPI (Python) OR Express (Node.js) — pick one and go deep
lessons.objective: Both are excellent. Pick the language you prefer and master that stack.

reading.name: FastAPI tutorial (best framework docs on the internet)

courses.title: FastAPI - The Complete Course (Eric Roby)
Node.js, Express, MongoDB & More: The Complete Bootcamp (Jonas Schmedtmann)

lessons.project: Build a backend for a notes app: create, read, update, delete notes. Connect to a real database.

lessons.title: Authentication: signup, login, sessions, JWT
lessons.objective: Let people have accounts. The most-asked feature in any real app.
lessons.project: Add user signup/login to your notes backend. Hashed passwords. Each user only sees their own notes.

lessons.title: Connect React to your backend
lessons.objective: Frontend + backend talk via HTTP. Now you have a complete app.
lessons.project: Build a React frontend for your notes API. Login, create notes, edit, delete. Deploy both.

lessons.title: Real-world full-stack project
lessons.objective: One project you'd put on a resume. Pick something you actually want to use.
lessons.project: Build something real: a habit tracker, a reading log, a workout planner, a study group finder. Anything you'd actually use. Deploy it. Get 5 friends to try it.

phases.title: Phase 8 — Shipping Software (Cloud, DevOps, Application Delivery)
phases.objective: Code on your laptop is worthless. Code running for users is everything. This phase covers the journey from 'works on my machine' to a real public app.
phases.outcome: You can deploy any app you build to a real URL with HTTPS, logging, and automatic updates on every commit.

lessons.title: AWS, GCP, Azure — what they actually are
lessons.objective: Three companies rent out 95% of the world's computing. Understand the basics of each.

courses.title: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner CLF-C02 (Stephane Maarek)

lessons.project: Sign up for AWS free tier. Spin up a t2.micro EC2. SSH into it. Stop and delete it. Don't get charged.

lessons.title: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS — the three layers
lessons.objective: Bare servers (IaaS), hosted environments (PaaS), full apps (SaaS). Pick your altitude.
lessons.project: Categorize 10 services you know: AWS EC2, Heroku, Vercel, Notion, Render, Salesforce, Kubernetes, GitHub, Spotify, Cloudflare. Each is which?

lessons.title: What is Docker? Why everyone uses it
lessons.objective: Package any app with all its dependencies into one runnable image. The unit of modern deployment.

courses.title: Docker & Kubernetes: The Practical Guide (Maximilian Schwarzmüller)

lessons.project: Containerize your full-stack project. Push the image to Docker Hub. Run it on someone else's machine to prove it works.

lessons.title: What does 'going to production' mean?
lessons.objective: The discipline of safely getting your code in front of users.
lessons.project: Watch any 'inside Spotify/Netflix engineering' deployment talk on YouTube. Take 1 page of notes.

lessons.title: Deploying for free: Vercel, Render, Fly.io
lessons.objective: Three excellent free hosting options for real apps.
lessons.project: Deploy your full-stack project to a real public URL on one of these. Share the link on LinkedIn.

lessons.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
lessons.objective: Auto-test and auto-deploy on every commit. Standard in 2026.
lessons.project: Set up a GitHub Action: on every PR to your project, run tests automatically. On merge to main, auto-deploy.

lessons.title: Logs, monitoring, and waking up at 3am
lessons.objective: How real engineers know when their app breaks before users complain.
lessons.project: Add Sentry (free) to your deployed app. Trigger a test error. Confirm you see it in the dashboard.

phases.earning_milestone: MILESTONE: With Phase 1–8 done you're a competent junior full-stack developer. You can credibly apply for: paid internships (₹40k–80k/mo or $1500–3000/mo at international startups), full-stack freelance projects ($30–60/hr), and your first product launch. This is roughly the 18–22 month mark.

phases.title: Phase 9 — CS Fundamentals & Interview Prep
phases.objective: The skills that get you past the technical interview at any serious company. Skip-able if your goal is freelance/indie. Critical if your goal is FAANG / top startup.
phases.outcome: You can solve LeetCode Easy comfortably and most Mediums. You understand system design fundamentals.

lessons.title: Big-O notation — measuring efficiency
lessons.objective: Why one solution can be 1000x faster than another with same output.
lessons.project: Write two versions of 'find duplicates in a list': O(n²) and O(n). Time them on lists of 100, 1k, 10k, 100k. Plot the difference.

lessons.title: Arrays, hashmaps, stacks, queues, linked lists
lessons.objective: The 5 data structures used in 90% of all code.

courses.title: Master the Coding Interview: Data Structures + Algorithms (Andrei Neagoie)
JavaScript Algorithms and Data Structures Masterclass (Colt Steele)

lessons.project: Solve the first 30 problems of NeetCode 150. Push solutions with explanations to a GitHub repo.

lessons.title: Trees, graphs, recursion
lessons.objective: The conceptual leap. Recursion is the single skill that unlocks everything else.
lessons.project: Solve 30 more NeetCode problems. Include 10 tree problems and 5 graph problems.

lessons.title: Dynamic programming (the boss fight)
lessons.objective: DP is the hardest interview topic. Practice early, practice often.
lessons.project: Solve 10 classic DP problems: climbing stairs, house robber, longest common subsequence, coin change, edit distance, etc.

lessons.title: How to design Twitter (or any large system)
lessons.objective: Senior interviews are 50% system design. Start now.
lessons.project: Whiteboard (paper is fine): how would YOU design Twitter? Address: feed, posting, follows, scale to 100M users. Compare your design to ByteByteGo's video. Iterate.

phases.title: Phase 10 — Math for Machine Learning
phases.objective: You don't need to be a mathematician. But to UNDERSTAND ML — not just call .fit() — you need linear algebra, calculus, and probability. Skip this and you'll be a tutorial coder forever.
phases.outcome: You can read an ML paper's equations and follow along. You understand WHY gradient descent works.

lessons.title: Vectors, matrices, dot products
lessons.objective: The language ML is written in. Every neural network is a chain of matrix multiplies.

reading.name: 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Linear Algebra (the single best math series ever made)

courses.title: Mathematics for Machine Learning Specialization (Imperial College London)

lessons.project: Implement matrix multiplication from scratch in Python (no NumPy). Verify against NumPy's output.

lessons.title: Eigenvectors, eigenvalues, PCA intuition
lessons.objective: The deeper layer. Used in dimensionality reduction, recommender systems.
lessons.project: Apply PCA (using sklearn) to a 100-dimensional dataset. Reduce to 2D. Plot.

lessons.title: Derivatives, chain rule, gradients
lessons.objective: Calculus is the engine of ML. Backpropagation IS the chain rule.
lessons.project: By hand, compute the gradient of f(x,y) = x²y + sin(y) at (1, π/2).

lessons.title: Probability, distributions, Bayes' theorem
lessons.objective: ML is applied probability. Master uncertainty.

courses.title: Statistics with Python Specialization (UMichigan)

lessons.project: Simulate rolling a die 10,000 times in Python. Plot the distribution. Verify mean is ~3.5.

phases.title: Phase 11 — Machine Learning Foundations
phases.objective: Classical ML — the algorithms behind 80% of real-world ML systems still in production. Learn this BEFORE jumping to deep learning.
phases.outcome: You can build, train, evaluate, and deploy a model end-to-end. 2 ML projects on GitHub.

lessons.title: Supervised vs unsupervised, train/test, overfitting
lessons.objective: The mental model every ML engineer carries.

courses.title: Machine Learning Specialization (Andrew Ng / DeepLearning.AI)
Machine Learning A-Z: AI, Python & R (Kirill Eremenko)

lessons.project: Train a model to predict housing prices on the California Housing dataset. Compare 3 algorithms.

lessons.title: The classical algorithm zoo: linear/logistic regression, trees, random forests, SVM, k-NN
lessons.objective: Each is still production-relevant in 2026.
lessons.project: Build a Titanic survival classifier on Kaggle. Aim for top 50%.

lessons.title: Feature engineering, cross-validation, model evaluation
lessons.objective: What separates good ML engineers from people who just call .fit().
lessons.project: Improve your Titanic score. Document every change and what it did to accuracy.

modules.title: Unsupervised learning

lessons.title: Clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection
lessons.objective: Find patterns without labels.
lessons.project: Cluster 10,000 customer records into 5 groups. Describe each segment.

modules.earning_milestone: MILESTONE: At this point you can apply for ML internships (₹50k–1.5L/mo in India, $3k–8k/mo internationally), freelance ML work on Upwork ($25–60/hr), or compete for Kaggle prize money. Polish your portfolio NOW.

phases.title: Phase 12 — Deep Learning, LLMs & Generative AI
phases.objective: The architectures behind ChatGPT, Claude, Stable Diffusion, and every AI product. The most leveraged skillset on Earth in 2026.
phases.outcome: You've built a neural network from scratch and a working RAG/agent app on top of LLMs.

lessons.title: Perceptrons, activations, backpropagation
lessons.objective: Build a neural net from scratch. Pure NumPy. No frameworks. This will change how you see AI forever.

reading.name: Andrej Karpathy — Neural Networks: Zero to Hero (free, the gold standard)
Neural Networks and Deep Learning (Michael Nielsen, free book)

courses.title: Deep Learning Specialization (Andrew Ng)

lessons.project: Implement Karpathy's micrograd. Train a tiny neural net on MNIST.

lessons.title: PyTorch — the dominant deep learning framework
lessons.objective: PyTorch has won. Industry, research, OpenAI, Anthropic — everyone uses it.

courses.title: PyTorch for Deep Learning Bootcamp (Daniel Bourke + Andrei Neagoie)

lessons.project: Reproduce your micrograd net in PyTorch. Train on FashionMNIST. Hit >88% accuracy.

lessons.title: CNNs — how AI sees
lessons.objective: Behind every image classifier, face recognition, self-driving system.
lessons.project: Train a CNN to classify 10 types of food images. >75% test accuracy.

lessons.title: Transformers & attention — the architecture behind ChatGPT/Claude
lessons.objective: The single most important paper of the 2010s. Understand it deeply.

reading.name: Andrej Karpathy — Let's build GPT (legendary 2-hour video)

courses.title: Generative AI with LLMs (DeepLearning.AI + AWS)

lessons.project: Following Karpathy's video, code a GPT from scratch. Train it on tinyshakespeare.

modules.title: Applied AI — building on top of LLMs

lessons.title: OpenAI / Anthropic / Hugging Face APIs
lessons.objective: Building products on top of frontier LLMs. What most AI engineers do day-to-day in 2026.

courses.title: All short courses — Prompt Engineering, LangChain, RAG

lessons.project: Build a chatbot that answers questions about a PDF you upload (basic RAG).

lessons.title: Prompt engineering, RAG, agents
lessons.objective: The applied AI engineer toolkit. These three skills get you hired.
lessons.project: Build an AI agent that takes a PDF resume + job description and writes a tailored cover letter. Deploy it.

lessons.title: Fine-tuning open-source models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen)
lessons.objective: Fine-tune your own LLM. Cheaper and more controllable than API calls.
lessons.project: Fine-tune a 7B model with LoRA on a domain you care about (sports stats, medicine, law, anime).

modules.earning_milestone: BIG MILESTONE: AI engineers with a real portfolio earn $40–120/hr on Upwork even before graduating university. Internships at AI startups: $5k–10k/month. Apply to: YC startups, DeepLearning.AI fellowships, Outlier.ai (AI training, $30–50/hr remote), Anthropic / OpenAI / Google residencies.

phases.title: Phase 13 — Career Launch & Side Income
phases.objective: Turn skills into income, reputation, and a real career.
phases.outcome: Public portfolio, paid work, and ideally a launched product.

lessons.title: Portfolio of 5 great projects
lessons.objective: Quality > quantity. 5 polished, deployed projects with great READMEs beat 30 half-finished tutorials.
lessons.project: Pick your best 5 projects. Rewrite the READMEs. Add screenshots, demo links, architecture diagrams. Pin them on GitHub.

lessons.title: Public learning: Twitter/X, LinkedIn, blog
lessons.objective: Recruiters Google your name. Make sure they find something. Public learning compounds insanely.
lessons.project: Write 1 short technical post per month. Topics: 'How I built X', 'How Y works under the hood'. Post on Twitter, LinkedIn, dev.to, your own blog.

lessons.title: Freelancing: Upwork, Fiverr, Toptal
lessons.objective: Trade hours for cash, build a track record, get testimonials.
lessons.project: Open Upwork + Fiverr + LinkedIn profiles. Apply to 10 jobs per week, every week.

lessons.title: Internships, residencies, full-time roles
lessons.objective: The career-defining moves.
lessons.project: Apply to 5 internships every cycle. FAANG, top startups, AI labs. Worst case: feedback.

lessons.title: Build a product (the long game)
lessons.objective: One indie product earning $1k/month at age 21 changes your entire trajectory.

reading.name: The Mom Test (book — talk to users)

lessons.project: Find ONE problem you have. Build a tiny tool that solves it. Post on Product Hunt and Reddit. Talk to your first users.
